retrieved_docs.title: Migraine With Aura -- Treatment / Management -- Calcitonin gene-related peptide antagonists
retrieved_docs.content: CGRP antagonists are reasonable options for patients who are unresponsive to the above options. They are available in parenteral, large molecules monoclonal antibodies targeting the CGRP receptor and oral small-molecule antagonists. Eptinezumab dosed 100 to 300 mg IV infusion once every 3 months Fremanezumab dosed 225 mg subcutaneously once monthly or 675 mg once every 3 months Atogepant 10, 30, or 60 mg orally once daily Rimegepant 75 mg orally every other day for prevention and 75 mg once daily for acute treatment

retrieved_docs.title: Migraine With Aura -- Treatment / Management -- Calcitonin gene-related peptide antagonists
retrieved_docs.content: Small molecule antagonists, or gepants, are oral agents proven effective for acute migraine treatment. Though more commonly used as a prophylactic therapy, some formulations are effective for abortive use. Rimegepant, administered as a single 75 mg dose, and ubrogepant are among the available options. CGRP antagonists are helpful for patients who do not respond to conventional therapy or those with coronary artery disease. [26] Rimegepant has approval for both acute and preventative treatment. [27] Zavegepant offers an intranasal option.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Calcitonin gene-related peptide receptors (CGRP) receptor antagonists and monoclonal antibodies against CGRP and its receptor are medications used in the management and treatment of migraine, which is the sixth-highest cause worldwide of years lost due to disability as per the Global Burden of Disease Study (2013). This activity describes the indications, actions, and contraindications for these drugs as valuable agents in the prophylaxis and acute management of migraine and cluster headaches. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, monitoring, adverse events, relevant interactions, and other key elements in the clinical setting as relates to the essential points needed by members of an interprofessional team managing the care of patients with migraines.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Summarize the role of RAMP1 in the formation of the CGRP receptor. Outline the currently FDA-approved drugs acting on CGRP or its receptor. Identify the most common adverse events associated with CGRP receptor antagonists. Review the importance of proper monitoring and coordination amongst interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients affected by migraine. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Headaches -- Treatment / Management -- Chronic Migraine
retrieved_docs.content: Monoclonal antibodies that target calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) are the newest development in chronic migraine treatment. Erenumab, fremanezumab, and galcanezumab are approved for chronic migraines, which have failed to respond to other treatments. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, eptinezumab, galcanezumab, fremanezumab) are used for migraine prophylaxis in episodic and chronic migraine. Rimegepant and ubrogepant are used for acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. galcanezumab and fremanezumab are under development for the preventive treatment of cluster headaches.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Since the studies regarding CGRP and its receptor, physiology, and pharmacology were conducted relatively recently, there is much unknown about it. The new drugs acting on the CGRP receptor have greater efficacy and advantages over the drugs currently used for the treatment of migraines, which may lead to the widespread use of these new drugs. However, professionals within the field of internal medicine and neurology have to take care while prescribing these drugs and properly inform patients about the potential side effects of these medications. Watching for adverse effects reported in previous studies and preparing for those that have not yet been described is also vital.

retrieved_docs.title: Migraine With Aura -- Treatment / Management -- Calcitonin gene-related peptide antagonists
retrieved_docs.content: Studies indicate that a trial of 3 to 6 months may be necessary to evaluate the full effect of some preventative medications. Avoid valproate in women of childbearing age and topiramate during pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: These monoclonal antibodies act rapidly and prevent the binding of CGRP. This receptor blockade is long-term, which leads to a reliable method of migraine prophylaxis using only a few doses of the drug per month. These drugs are degraded into small peptides and amino acids by enzymatic proteolysis before excretion, as with other monoclonal antibodies.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: The first four drugs listed are monoclonal antibodies used in the prophylaxis of chronic and episodic migraine.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: CGRP receptor antagonists ("gepants") are the first class of migraine-specific medication that does not have vasoconstrictive action, shows similar efficacy, has fewer adverse effects, and has a possibly longer period of activity than triptans. [7] Gepants demonstrate an extremely high affinity for CGRP receptors of human and non-human primates, preventing in this way the interaction between CGRP and its receptor. [12] The structured N-terminal extracellular (ECD) domain of CLR serves as a binding site for gepants, thus providing its fast-acting antagonistic action. The currently FDA-approved gepants are rimegepant and ubrogepant.

retrieved_docs.title: Menstrual-Related Headache -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacologic modulation of CGRP activity appears to mediate trigeminovascular pain transmission in migraine. CGRP antagonists are novel options for acute migraine treatment in patients with either insufficient response or contraindications to first-line medications. [17] [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Migraine Medications -- Function -- Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide (CGRP)
retrieved_docs.content: CGRP monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) are the only class of currently used preventives explicitly developed for the treatment of migraines. The current thinking is that CGRP mediates the vasodilatory component of neurogenic inflammation, as CGRP is a widely distributed vasodilator. The CGRP mAbs target either the CGRP molecule itself or the CGRP receptor. In network meta-analysis, the CGRP mAbs seemed to be as effective as other preventive treatments, but have fewer side effects. [14] Long-term data on safety, however, is limited.  These medications include erenumab, fremanezumab, and galcanezumab.

retrieved_docs.title: Migraine-Associated Vertigo  -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A recent study shows the effectiveness of monoclonal antibodies targeting calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) in treating VM, with the authors suggesting early initiation of this treatment for patients with VM. CGRP inhibition may be one of the most effective preventive therapies available. [34] However, significant challenges associated with CGRP as a treatment option are its high cost and patients' difficulty gaining insurance approval to receive these medications.

retrieved_docs.title: Migraine Prophylaxis -- Function -- 1. Pharmacological Therapies for Migraine Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: U.S. FDA approved Erenumab for the treatment of migraine prevention in May 2018. It is a monoclonal antibody that mediates the transmission of migraine pain by binding to the calcitonin gene-related peptide receptor. It comes as a monthly subcutaneous injection. Common side effects include injection site reaction, constipation, and cramps. [3] [25] Others

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, FDA-approved drugs acting on the CGRP, or its receptor, are those used in the treatment or prophylaxis of migraine. This group includes monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, eptinezumab, galcanezumab, fremanezumab) against the CGRP receptor and CGRP receptor antagonists (rimegepant and ubrogepant). In addition, atogepant and zavegepant are other receptor antagonists that have received FDA approval.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 335
retrieved_docs.content: Substance with peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action represents a broad class of pharmacologic agents that block the activity of peptide hormone receptors. Within this overarching category, Substance with receptor antagonist mechanism of action (substance) is identified as a more specific subclass of agents that exert their therapeutic effect by antagonizing receptors in general. A particular example of this subclass is antagonisté CGRP receptorů, which comprises compounds that specifically inhibit the calcitonin gene‑related peptide (CGRP) receptor. Thus, CGRP receptor antagonists are situated under the umbrella of receptor‑antagonist substances, which in turn are encompassed by the larger group of peptide‑hormone receptor antagonists. This hierarchical organization clarifies how these drug classes relate to one another and aids clinicians in understanding the scope of receptor‑targeted therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
retrieved_docs.content: Antimigraine preparations comprise a diverse group of medications used to prevent or treat migraine attacks. This category includes the oral‑dose product of clonidine, other antimigraine agents classified in the ATC system, and specific drugs such as almotriptan, naratriptan, methysergid, atogepant, dichloralphenazone, lisurid, and pizotylin. These agents vary in their mechanisms of action—triptans like almotriptan, naratriptan, and methysergid act as serotonin receptor agonists, atogepant is a CGRP receptor antagonist, dichloralphenazone provides analgesic and sedative effects, and clonidine, lisurid, and pizotylin have additional vasoconstrictive or neuromodulatory properties. Together, they offer clinicians multiple therapeutic options for individualized migraine management and improve patient care by targeting different pathophysiological pathways involved in migraine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 643
retrieved_docs.content: Antimigraine preparations comprise a diverse group of pharmacologic agents used to treat migraine, including clonidine formulated for oral dosing, the broader category of other ATC‑classified antimigraine drugs, and specific compounds such as almotriptan, atogepant, dichloralphenazone, flunarizin‑dihydrochloride, lisuride, methysergide, naratriptan, and the alkaloid subclass námelové alkaloidy. Pizotylin is also classified within this therapeutic class. Together, these agents represent various mechanisms of action—triptans (almotriptan, naratriptan), CGRP receptor antagonists (atogepant), serotonin‑related agents (lisuride, methysergide), and other migraine‑targeted compounds—providing clinicians with multiple options for individualized migraine management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1710
retrieved_docs.content: Ergotamine tartrate is a pharmacologic agent that may be used to treat headache, including migraine. By acting as an ergot alkaloid, ergotamine tartrate can alleviate acute migraine attacks through vasoconstriction of intracranial blood vessels and inhibition of neurogenic inflammation, providing symptomatic relief for patients experiencing these types of headache.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
retrieved_docs.content: Icatibant is classified among the drugs used to treat hereditary angioedema, a rare genetic disorder characterized by episodic swelling of the skin and mucosal tissues. As a bradykinin B2‑receptor antagonist, icatibant specifically blocks the action of bradykinin, a key mediator of the swelling attacks in hereditary angioedema, thereby providing rapid relief of symptoms during acute episodes. Inclusion of icatibant in the therapeutic armamentarium underscores its role as a targeted pharmacologic option for managing this condition and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 68
retrieved_docs.content: Antimigraine preparations comprise a diverse group of pharmacologic agents used to prevent or treat migraine attacks. Among these agents are oral clonidine products, which are classified as antimigraine preparations, as well as other antimigraine preparations listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system. Specific triptans such as almotriptan, naratriptan, and the zolmitriptan 2.5 mg oral tablet are also members of this class, and the zolmitriptan tablet contains the inactive ingredient hypromelóza. Hypromelóza, besides serving as an excipient in zolmitriptan, is linked to nasal vasoconstrictor formulations, reflecting its broader relationship to vasoconstrictive agents used in the nasal cavity. Additional antimigraine drugs include atogepant, dichloralphenazone, flunarizin‑dihydrochloride, lisurid, methysergid, and pizotylin, all of which are recognized as types of antimigraine preparations. The category further encompasses a sub‑class of alkaloid compounds referred to as “námelové alkaloidy,” highlighting the chemical diversity within migraine therapy. Together, these agents illustrate the wide range of mechanisms—ranging from serotonin receptor agonism to CGRP antagonism and vasoconstriction—employed to manage migraine, underscoring the importance of selecting appropriate formulations and excipients for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 361
retrieved_docs.content: Antimigraine preparations constitute a therapeutic class that includes a diverse range of agents used to treat migraine attacks. Within this class are the oral‑dose formulation of clonidine marketed as “Clonidine only product in oral dose form,” the broader category of “Other antimigraine preparations in ATC,” and the specific triptan almotriptan. Additional members of the antimigraine preparations class are atogepant, dichloralphenazone, flunarizin‑dihydrochlorid, lisurid, methysergid, and naratriptan. All of these compounds share the common purpose of alleviating migraine symptoms, either through acute relief or preventive mechanisms, and are therefore grouped together under the umbrella of antimigraine preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 413
retrieved_docs.content: conditions. Overall, the integrated knowledge base links migraine (شقيقة) to its hierarchical placement under headache and pain, its clinical overlap with vascular and hereditary headache disorders, its manifestation in specific cerebrovascular diseases, and its prevention with topiramat, thereby providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing migraine within the spectrum of nervous system disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1062
retrieved_docs.content: within nervous system disorders. Clinically, شقيقة and شقيقة فالجية عائلية are considered clinically similar, and شقيقة and صداع وعائي are described as related and possibly synonymous, suggesting substantial overlap in their clinical presentation and underlying mechanisms. Together, these relationships integrate migraine into a network of genetic, vascular, and systemic neurological conditions, underscore its diagnostic and therapeutic considerations, and illustrate its connections to both broader headache classifications and specific hereditary migraine phenotypes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 68
retrieved_docs.content: Clonidine only product in oral dose form, other antimigraine preparations in ATC, almotriptan, atogepant, dichloralphenazone, flunarizin‑dihydrochlorid, lisurid, methysergid, and naratriptan are all classified as antimigraine preparations, representing a diverse group of pharmacologic agents used to prevent or treat migraine attacks. Within this therapeutic class, the subgroup known as “námelové alkaloidy” is identified as a child category, indicating that certain alkaloid‑derived compounds also belong to the broader antimigraine preparations family. By grouping these varied medications under the single umbrella of antimigraine preparations, clinicians can recognize their shared therapeutic purpose while also appreciating the distinct mechanisms and chemical structures—ranging from triptans (almotriptan, naratriptan) to gepants (atogepant) and other agents such as dichloralphenazone and flunarizin‑dihydrochlorid—that contribute to migraine management. This integrated classification supports comprehensive patient care by facilitating selection of appropriate agents based on individual response, side‑effect profiles, and specific migraine characteristics.
